text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
INTERVAL SURVEILLANCE VISIT

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
AGE: 48-49 years
DIAGNOSIS: High-grade serous carcinoma, bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0)
GERMLINE STATUS: BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC; 5382insC founder mutation)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Routinely scheduled surveillance visit. Patient presenting for interval follow-up approximately 30 months from initial diagnosis and 22 months on niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy. She reports a new symptom of mild right-sided flank discomfort of approximately two weeks' duration.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 48-to-49-year-old woman of Ghanaian descent with a well-established diagnosis of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, identified approximately 30 months ago. Her clinical course to date has been encouraging. She underwent primary debulking surgery achieving complete gross resection (R0) at the time of diagnosis, followed by six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) and paclitaxel (dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5 and 6 due to cumulative peripheral neuropathy), completing adjuvant chemotherapy approximately 22 months ago. She achieved a complete biochemical and radiographic response, with CA-125 normalizing to 11 U/mL post-treatment and surveillance imaging confirming no evidence of disease. She was subsequently initiated on niraparib maintenance therapy at 200 mg daily, which required dose reduction to 100 mg daily approximately three weeks into treatment due to Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelets nadir 58,000/uL). She has been maintained on niraparib 100 mg daily since that time with Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia that has remained stable and manageable. She developed niraparib-associated hypertension, now well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg daily.

At the prior surveillance visit (approximately 24 months from diagnosis, 16 months on niraparib), she was doing exceptionally well with CA-125 of 7 U/mL and CBC demonstrating platelets at 114,000/uL. She reported good functional status, return to full-time teaching, and involvement in yoga and outdoor activities. Her surveillance CT at 24 months post-chemotherapy showed no evidence of disease recurrence, sustained complete remission.

Since that visit, she now presents with a new complaint of mild right flank discomfort. She describes it as a dull, intermittent ache in the right costovertebral angle and right lower back region, present for approximately two weeks, non-radiating. She rates it a 3/10 in severity. She denies fever, chills, rigors, or nausea/vomiting. She reports no dysuria or urinary urgency, but does note that her urine appeared slightly pink on one occasion approximately one week ago, which she describes as a single episode that self-resolved. She has not noted recurrence of discolored urine since. She denies any new abdominal pain, bloating, change in bowel habits, early satiety, or unintentional weight loss. She specifically denies the constellation of symptoms she experienced at initial presentation (progressive abdominal distension, pelvic pressure, significant weight loss). She denies shortness of breath, cough, or hemoptysis. She has had no falls or trauma to the flank region.

No new medications started since the last visit. She remains on niraparib 100 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, duloxetine 30 mg daily (for residual chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy), and a multivitamin. She has been compliant with her niraparib as instructed, taking it at bedtime.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Right flank/costovertebral angle discomfort (mild, 2 weeks). Single episode of pink-tinged urine approximately one week ago, self-resolved.
Negative: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No weight loss or anorexia. No new abdominal pain, bloating, early satiety, or nausea. No change in bowel habits, no constipation or diarrhea. No dysuria, no urinary frequency beyond baseline, no urinary urgency, no frank hematuria currently. No shortness of breath or pleuritic chest pain. No lower extremity edema. No vaginal bleeding or discharge. No headache (mild occasional headaches previously noted on niraparib, now resolved). No new musculoskeletal symptoms. No palpitations. No peripheral neuropathy symptoms beyond prior Grade 1 stable tingling in bilateral feet. No petechiae, bruising, or bleeding from any site.

---

PAST MEDICAL AND SURGICAL HISTORY (SUMMARY)

- High-grade serous ovarian/fallopian tube carcinoma, FIGO IIIC, BRCA1-associated, as described
- Primary debulking surgery with R0 resection (TAH-BSO, omentectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, diaphragmatic stripping)
- Carboplatin hypersensitivity (Grade 2, cycle 2) -- managed with 12-step desensitization protocol for subsequent cycles
- Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy, Grade 2, improving to Grade 1 on duloxetine
- Niraparib-associated Grade 3 thrombocytopenia requiring dose reduction
- Niraparib-associated hypertension, now controlled on amlodipine
- No prior history of nephrolithiasis
- No prior genitourinary history of note
- Nonsmoker; no significant alcohol use

FAMILY HISTORY (RELEVANT): Maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 38; maternal grandmother with ovarian cancer at age 55. Patient has a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC). Patient's daughter (age 22) recently confirmed to be a BRCA1 carrier; referred for risk-reduction counseling.

ALLERGIES: Carboplatin (hypersensitivity, Grade 2 -- managed with desensitization; not a contraindication to further treatment with protocol)

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
1. Niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime
2. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily
3. Duloxetine (Cymbalta) 30 mg orally once daily
4. Ondansetron 8 mg orally as needed for nausea
5. Multivitamin once daily

SOCIAL HISTORY: Secondary school mathematics teacher, currently working full time. Mother of two adult children. Emigrated from Ghana at age 22. Nonsmoker; no alcohol or illicit substance use. Physically active; participates in yoga, hiking, and walking. Strong social support network; active in oncology support group.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented, conversational, pleasant.
Vital signs: Blood pressure 126/80 mmHg (well controlled compared to prior readings; on amlodipine 10 mg). Heart rate 74 bpm and regular. Temperature 36.8 degrees C. Respiratory rate 14/min. Oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight 63.5 kg (stable, no significant change from prior visits).
ECOG Performance Status: 0 (fully active, no restrictions).
Head and neck: No supraclavicular, cervical, or submandibular lymphadenopathy. No temporal wasting. Anicteric sclerae.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezing, or decreased breath sounds. No dullness to percussion at bases.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Well-healed midline laparotomy scar. No tenderness to palpation diffusely. No hepatosplenomegaly by palpation. No palpable masses. No fluid wave or shifting dullness to suggest ascites. Bowel sounds normoactive. No Murphy's sign.
Flank/costovertebral angle: Right CVA tenderness present to deep percussion, mild. Left CVA tenderness absent. No overlying skin changes or swelling.
Pelvic (limited external exam): No inguinal lymphadenopathy. No abnormal masses palpable in the lower abdomen or pelvis. Vault well-healed (patient has had hysterectomy).
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No petechiae or purpura. No bruising.
Neurological: Alert, oriented. Cranial nerves intact. Mild residual decreased sensation to light touch in bilateral toes (Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, stable and symmetric, non-progressive).
Skin: No rashes, petechiae, or skin lesions of concern.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CA-125: 10 U/mL (normal range <35 U/mL; persistently well within normal range, consistent with complete and sustained biochemical remission)
HE4: 51 pmol/L (normal)

CBC with differential:
- WBC: 5.0 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.9 g/dL (mild, stable; Grade 1 anemia; chronic on niraparib, improved from nadir of 8.9 during active chemotherapy)
- Hematocrit: 36%
- Platelets: 118,000/uL (Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia; stable and chronic on niraparib 100 mg; no trend toward further decline compared to recent checks at 114,000 and 109,000)
- ANC: 2.8 x10^9/L (normal)
- No blasts, no atypical lymphocytes on differential

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L, Chloride: 103 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 25 mEq/L
- BUN: 18 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.83 mg/dL, eGFR: >60 mL/min/1.73m2 (stable renal function)
- AST: 22 U/L, ALT: 19 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L, Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL (liver function normal)
- Albumin: 4.1 g/dL
- Glucose: 94 mg/dL

Urinalysis (in-office dipstick):
- Color: yellow, clear
- Specific gravity: 1.018
- pH: 6.0
- Leukocyte esterase: negative
- Nitrites: negative
- Protein: trace
- Blood/hemoglobin: trace positive (1+)
- No glucose, no ketones, no bilirubin
Comment: Trace hematuria on dipstick without pyuria or bacteriuria. No clinical features to suggest urinary tract infection. Hematuria in the context of right flank pain warrants further evaluation. Notably, no active macroscopic hematuria today.

---

IMAGING

CT urogram ordered today given right flank pain and trace hematuria -- to evaluate for urolithiasis, upper tract urothelial lesion, or other genitourinary pathology, and to simultaneously provide assessment of the abdomen/pelvis for any evidence of disease recurrence (though CA-125 remains normal and clinical suspicion is low). Please refer to separate imaging report.

Prior surveillance CT (approximately 6 months ago, 24 months post-chemo): No evidence of disease, no peritoneal implants, no lymphadenopathy, no ascites. Complete remission confirmed.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN/FALLOPIAN TUBE CARCINOMA, FIGO IIIC, BRCA1-ASSOCIATED -- SUSTAINED COMPLETE REMISSION

Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues to do extremely well at 30 months from her initial diagnosis. CA-125 remains well within the normal range at 10 U/mL, and there are no clinical signs or symptoms to suggest disease recurrence. She has no constitutional symptoms, no abdominal distension, no new lymphadenopathy, and no pelvic masses on examination. Her performance status is ECOG 0 -- she is fully active and back to her normal life including teaching and physical activities. This is a very reassuring clinical picture for a patient with stage IIIC disease who achieved R0 resection, complete pathologic and biochemical remission, and has now been on maintenance PARP inhibitor therapy for 22 months.

Given her germline BRCA1 status, high HRD score, and complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, she remains at high risk for eventual recurrence but has an appreciably better prognosis than BRCA1-wild-type patients. The sustained remission at 30 months is consistent with the outcomes data from SOLO-1, where germline BRCA1/2 patients maintained on olaparib had significantly prolonged progression-free survival. We anticipate continuing niraparib toward the planned 3-year maintenance duration.

Plan:
- Continue niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg daily as planned
- CA-125 and CBC every 3 months
- Next surveillance CT at approximately 36 months from diagnosis (in approximately 6 months)
- Annual breast surveillance (MRI and mammography) per BRCA1 carrier protocol; patient instructed to follow up with her breast imaging center; prior breast MRI at an outside institution reportedly normal

2. NEW RIGHT FLANK PAIN AND TRACE HEMATURIA -- LIKELY UROLITHIASIS

The presentation of mild right CVA tenderness, one episode of pink-tinged urine, and trace hematuria on urinalysis in the absence of pyuria or fever is most consistent with urolithiasis. There is no clinical or biochemical evidence to suggest this is related to her ovarian cancer. The right kidney was not involved in her original disease (no parenchymal metastases, no hydronephrosis on prior imaging), and her CA-125 remains normal. The absence of fever, chills, or signs of systemic illness argues against pyelonephritis. Differential diagnosis includes nephrolithiasis (most likely), less likely a small upper tract urothelial lesion (though this would be unusual given her age and lack of risk factors), and renal cyst or other benign finding.

Plan:
- CT urogram ordered today (non-contrast CT kidneys/ureters/bladder with contrast phase for evaluation of the collecting system) -- results pending at time of this note; see separate radiology report
- Urology consultation placed for further evaluation and management pending CT findings
- If consistent with nephrolithiasis: conservative management with increased oral hydration (target >2-3 L water daily), tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily for alpha-blockade to facilitate stone passage, and ibuprofen PRN for pain (with caution given thrombocytopenia -- patient counseled to minimize NSAID use; acetaminophen 500-1000 mg every 6 hours PRN preferred given platelet count)
- Patient instructed to strain urine to capture stone if passed, for composition analysis
- Patient to contact clinic or proceed to emergency department if pain acutely worsens, she develops fever, or if unable to urinate
- Repeat urinalysis in 4-6 weeks to confirm resolution of hematuria

3. NIRAPARIB MAINTENANCE -- ONGOING TOLERABILITY MONITORING

Niraparib 100 mg daily has been well-tolerated at this reduced dose. Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia (platelets 118,000/uL) is chronic and stable, with no trend toward further decline. No active bleeding or bruising. Hemoglobin is mildly low at 11.9 g/dL but stable and not symptomatic.

Regarding NSAIDs and aspirin in the context of thrombocytopenia: patient reminded to avoid ibuprofen and aspirin as first-line analgesics given platelet count. Acetaminophen preferred for analgesia. If NSAID use becomes necessary for stone pain, limit to short course with close monitoring and platelet check.

Plan:
- Continue niraparib 100 mg daily
- CBC with platelets in 4 weeks, or sooner if any bleeding symptoms develop
- No dose modification planned at this time given stable Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia

4. NIRAPARIB-ASSOCIATED HYPERTENSION -- WELL CONTROLLED

Blood pressure today 126/80 mmHg. Well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg daily. No changes to antihypertensive regimen at this time.

Plan:
- Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily
- Home blood pressure monitoring encouraged; patient reminded to log readings
- Follow up with primary care physician for ongoing hypertension management

5. CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY -- STABLE, RESOLVING

Patient's peripheral neuropathy, which peaked at Grade 2 during active chemotherapy (bilateral hands and feet), has continued to improve on duloxetine. Currently Grade 1, mild tingling limited to bilateral toes, non-limiting and stable. No progression noted.

Plan:
- Continue duloxetine 30 mg daily
- Reassess at next visit; may consider tapering duloxetine if neuropathy continues to improve

6. BREAST CANCER SURVEILLANCE -- GERMLINE BRCA1 CARRIER

Given her germline BRCA1 status (c.5266dupC founder variant), Ms. Osei-Bonsu carries a substantially elevated lifetime risk of breast cancer. She is currently receiving appropriate breast surveillance with annual breast MRI and mammography per high-risk guidelines (National Comprehensive Cancer Network). She declined risk-reducing mastectomy at the 36-month visit discussion.

Plan:
- Continue annual breast MRI and screening mammography per BRCA1 high-risk protocol
- Reinforce importance of annual breast surveillance at each visit
- Discussion of risk-reducing options (mastectomy, chemoprevention) to continue at future visits as appropriate
- Patient's daughter confirmed BRCA1 carrier; has been referred for risk-reduction counseling

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL WELLBEING

Patient continues to be emotionally well and engaged in her oncology support group. She reports high quality of life, has returned to teaching and to physical activities including yoga and hiking. She remains motivated and adherent to her treatment plan. No new psychosocial concerns identified today.

Plan:
- Continued engagement with oncology support group
- Supportive counseling regarding her daughter's BRCA1 status if needed; this has been an emotionally significant development and family counseling resources offered

---

FOLLOW-UP

Patient to return in 4-6 weeks for repeat CBC (thrombocytopenia monitoring), urinalysis (hematuria follow-up), and review of CT urogram results with urology input. Routine oncology surveillance visit at 3 months for CA-125, CBC, and clinical assessment. Next surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis planned at approximately 36 months from initial diagnosis (approximately 6 months from today).

Patient instructed to call the office immediately for: worsening flank pain, fever, inability to urinate, new abdominal distension, new bleeding or bruising, or any other new or concerning symptoms.

Time spent: 45 minutes (history, examination, coordination of care, counseling and discussion with patient).

Electronically signed.
